From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Period of symptoms due to excessive or synchronous neuronal brain activity

This article is about epileptic seizures. For other types of seizures, see [Non-epileptic seizure](/wiki/Non-epileptic_seizure "Non-epileptic seizure"). For other uses, see [Seizure (disambiguation)](/wiki/Seizure_(disambiguation) "Seizure (disambiguation)").

A **seizure** is a sudden disruption of [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain") activity caused by excessive, synchronized [neuronal firing](/wiki/Action_potential "Action potential") that results in changes in behavior.[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11)[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12) This neurological condition is common, affecting approximately 50 million individuals around the world.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Depending on the regions of the brain involved, seizures can lead to changes in [movement](/wiki/Motor_skill "Motor skill"), [sensation](/wiki/Sensory_system "Sensory system"), [behavior](/wiki/Behavior "Behavior"), [awareness](/wiki/Awareness "Awareness"), or [consciousness](/wiki/Consciousness "Consciousness"). Symptoms vary widely. Some seizures involve subtle changes, such as brief lapses in attention or awareness (as seen in [absence seizures](/wiki/Absence_seizures "Absence seizures")), while others cause generalized convulsions with loss of consciousness ([tonic–clonic seizures](/wiki/Tonic%E2%80%93clonic_seizures "Tonic–clonic seizures")).[[15]](#cite_note-Berkowitz2022-15) Most seizures last less than two minutes and are followed by a [postictal period](/wiki/Postictal_state "Postictal state") of confusion, fatigue, or other symptoms.[[16]](#cite_note-Pottkämper2020-16) [Status epilepticus](/wiki/Status_epilepticus "Status epilepticus") is a medical emergency consisting of a seizure that lasts longer than five minutes, or multiple seizures without full recovery between episodes.[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Seizures are classified as provoked when they are triggered by a known cause such as fever, acute head trauma, or metabolic imbalance. Unprovoked seizures occur when no immediate trigger is identified. Recurrent unprovoked seizures define the neurological condition [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy").[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Clinical features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Clinical features")]

The clinical signs and symptoms associated with a seizure are also referred to as seizure semiology.[[19]](#cite_note-:5-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Seizure semiology varies depending on the brain regions involved and the type of seizure. This may affect [movement](/wiki/Motor_skills "Motor skills"), [sensation](/wiki/Sensory_system "Sensory system"), [autonomic functions](/wiki/Autonomic_nervous_system "Autonomic nervous system"), or [cognitive](/wiki/Cognition "Cognition") and emotional processing.[[19]](#cite_note-:5-19)[[21]](#cite_note-:6-21) Motor symptoms can include muscle stiffening (tonic activity), rhythmic jerking (clonic activity), sudden muscle jerks ([myoclonus](/wiki/Myoclonus "Myoclonus")), sudden loss of muscle tone ([atonia](/wiki/Atonia "Atonia")), eye deviation, or other repetitive involuntary movements ([automatisms](/wiki/Automatism_(medicine) "Automatism (medicine)")).[[19]](#cite_note-:5-19)[[22]](#cite_note-22) Sensory disturbances may involve [tingling](/wiki/Tingling "Tingling"), visual phenomena, or [hallucinated sounds](/wiki/Auditory_hallucination "Auditory hallucination").[[19]](#cite_note-:5-19)[[23]](#cite_note-23) Autonomic features can include changes in heart rate, respiration, or gastrointestinal sensations.[[21]](#cite_note-:6-21)[[24]](#cite_note-24) Cognitive or emotional symptoms may manifest as confusion, fear, or altered perception.[[19]](#cite_note-:5-19)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Some individuals experience an [aura](/wiki/Aura_(symptom) "Aura (symptom)") associated with their focal seizures, characterized by subjective sensations such as unusual smells, a sudden emotional shift, or feelings of [déjà vu](/wiki/D%C3%A9j%C3%A0_vu "Déjà vu").[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Most seizures last less than two minutes and may be followed by a recovery phase known as the [postictal state](/wiki/Postictal_state "Postictal state"), which may include confusion, fatigue, or other neurologic symptoms.[[16]](#cite_note-Pottkämper2020-16) Seizures lasting more than five minutes, or occurring in rapid succession without recovery, are classified as [status epilepticus](/wiki/Status_epilepticus "Status epilepticus"), a medical emergency that can result in long-term brain injury or death.[[15]](#cite_note-Berkowitz2022-15)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Classification")]

See also: [Seizure types](/wiki/Seizure_types "Seizure types")

Seizures are classified according to their site of onset in the brain, clinical features, and level of consciousness during the episode. In 2025, the [International League Against Epilepsy](/wiki/International_League_Against_Epilepsy "International League Against Epilepsy") (ILAE) released an updated classification to improve clarity, clinical relevance, and global applicability. The system distinguishes four major types: focal, generalized, unknown whether focal or generalized, and unclassified seizures. Seizures are further characterized based on whether consciousness is preserved or impaired, as determined by responsiveness during the event.[[28]](#cite_note-Beniczky2025-28)

### Focal seizures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Focal seizures")]

[Focal seizures](/wiki/Focal_seizure "Focal seizure") (previously known as partial seizures) originate within a network limited to one [hemisphere](/wiki/Cerebral_hemisphere "Cerebral hemisphere") of the brain.[[29]](#cite_note-29) They may arise from the cerebral cortex or subcortical structures. For a given seizure type, seizure usually starts in the same part of the brain each time. Once initiated, the seizure may remain localized or spread to adjacent areas, and in some cases, may travel to the opposite hemisphere (contralateral spread). Despite this potential for spread, the initial focus remains consistent.

Focal seizures are subdivided based on whether consciousness is preserved or impaired, a classifier defined by awareness and responsiveness during the event.[[28]](#cite_note-Beniczky2025-28) They are also differentiated by the localization or spread of epileptiform activity. The three major classifications of focal seizures are described below:[[28]](#cite_note-Beniczky2025-28)

* Focal preserved consciousness seizure: the person remains aware and responsive.
* Focal impaired consciousness seizure: awareness and/or responsiveness are affected.
* Focal-to-bilateral tonic-clonic seizures: abnormal brain activity spreads from one area to both brain hemispheres; awareness and/or responsiveness are affected.

Focal seizures can manifest with motor, sensory, autonomic, cognitive, or emotional symptoms, depending on the regions involved.[[28]](#cite_note-Beniczky2025-28) They often present with unilateral symptoms, but bilateral symptoms may also be seen, even when a seizure is not classified as focal-to-bilateral. This is because several structures in the brain have bilateral effects in the body (e.g. [primary visual cortex](/wiki/Visual_cortex "Visual cortex")).[[30]](#cite_note-:7-30)[[31]](#cite_note-:8-31)

Focal impaired consciousness seizures are the most common type of seizure in individuals older than one year of age, affecting 36% of epilepsy patients overall.[[30]](#cite_note-:7-30)

### Generalized seizures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Generalized seizures")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Gen-20s_SWK.png/500px-Gen-20s_SWK.png)](/wiki/File:Gen-20s_SWK.png)

Cutout from a 20s long generalized spike-wave-complex (SWC) seen on EEG.

[Generalized seizures](/wiki/Generalized_epilepsy "Generalized epilepsy") originate at a specific point within, and quickly spread across both hemispheres through interconnected brain networks.

They can be divided into three broad categories:[[28]](#cite_note-Beniczky2025-28)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

* [Absence seizures](/wiki/Absence_seizure "Absence seizure"): involve brief lapses in awareness
* [Generalized tonic–clonic seizures](/wiki/Generalized_tonic%E2%80%93clonic_seizure "Generalized tonic–clonic seizure"): involve stiffening (tonic phase) followed by rhythmic jerking (clonic phase)
* Other generalized seizures: encompass various motor and non-motor types

These categories include several subtypes of seizures, each with their own distinctive symptoms and diagnostic criteria.[[28]](#cite_note-Beniczky2025-28)

Like focal seizures, generalized seizures can present with asymmetric or bilateral symptoms, so thorough evaluation and history-gathering is necessary for diagnosis. One key difference between generalized and focal seizures is the age at onset, which is significantly lower in patients with generalized seizures.[[31]](#cite_note-:8-31)

Generalized tonic–clonic seizures, previously known as grand mal seizures, are associated with the highest morbidity and mortality. They are the primary risk factor for [sudden unexpected death in epilepsy](/wiki/Sudden_unexpected_death_in_epilepsy "Sudden unexpected death in epilepsy") (SUDEP).[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Unknown (whether focal or generalized) seizures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Unknown (whether focal or generalized) seizures")]

When available information is insufficient to determine whether a seizure is focal or generalized, it is classified as unknown. These seizures can be classified by level of consciousness and observable symptoms when possible.[[28]](#cite_note-Beniczky2025-28)

### Unclassified seizures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Unclassified seizures")]

Seizures are designated as unclassified when they are recognized as epileptic events, but insufficient information is available to assign them to any specific class. This is typically a temporary designation pending further clinical evaluation.[[28]](#cite_note-Beniczky2025-28)

## Causes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Causes")]

Main article: [Causes of seizures](/wiki/Causes_of_seizures "Causes of seizures")

Seizures can occur for many reasons and are broadly classified based on whether they are provoked (acute symptomatic) or unprovoked. Identifying the underlying cause is critical for guiding treatment and assessing the risk of recurrence.[[15]](#cite_note-Berkowitz2022-15)

### Provoked seizures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Provoked seizures")]

Provoked seizures are also known as acute symptomatic seizures. They are caused by an identifiable, transient condition affecting brain function. The temporal relationship between the cause and the seizure is important, but no single time frame has been established.[[34]](#cite_note-:9-34) Proposed criteria for provoked seizures include symptoms within one week of acute brain injury, following subdural hematoma, during central nervous system infection, or within 24 hours of a severe metabolic imbalance.[[34]](#cite_note-:9-34) Common causes of acute symptomatic seizures include:

* Metabolic disturbances: such as [hypoglycemia](/wiki/Hypoglycemia "Hypoglycemia") (low blood sugar), [hyponatremia](/wiki/Hyponatremia "Hyponatremia") (low sodium), [hypomagnesemia](/wiki/Magnesium_deficiency "Magnesium deficiency") (low magnesium), [hypocalcemia](/wiki/Hypocalcemia "Hypocalcemia") (low calcium), or [uremia](/wiki/Uremia "Uremia").[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3)[[35]](#cite_note-Nardone2016-35)
* Central nervous system infections: including [meningitis](/wiki/Meningitis "Meningitis"), [encephalitis](/wiki/Encephalitis "Encephalitis"), or [neurocysticercosis](/wiki/Neurocysticercosis "Neurocysticercosis").[[15]](#cite_note-Berkowitz2022-15)
* Acute brain injuries: such as [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke"), [traumatic brain injury](/wiki/Traumatic_brain_injury "Traumatic brain injury"), or [hemorrhage](/wiki/Hemorrhage "Hemorrhage").[[15]](#cite_note-Berkowitz2022-15)
* Substance-related factors: including [alcohol withdrawal](/wiki/Alcohol_withdrawal "Alcohol withdrawal"), [drug intoxication](/wiki/Substance_intoxication "Substance intoxication"), medication withdrawal, or certain [toxins.](/wiki/Toxin "Toxin")[[36]](#cite_note-Chen2015-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)
* Fever: particularly in children, leading to [febrile seizures](/wiki/Febrile_seizure "Febrile seizure").[[15]](#cite_note-Berkowitz2022-15)[[38]](#cite_note-Xixis2024-38)[[39]](#cite_note-Graves2012-39)

### Unprovoked seizures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Unprovoked seizures")]

Unprovoked seizures occur without an immediate precipitating event. These include spontaneous seizures and [reflex seizures](/wiki/Reflex_seizure "Reflex seizure"), which are consistently triggered by specific stimuli (e.g., flashing lights) but arise due to an enduring predisposition, not a transient cause.[[40]](#cite_note-Berg2010-40)

They typically reflect an underlying neurological predisposition and are associated with a higher risk of recurrence.[[15]](#cite_note-Berkowitz2022-15) Diagnostic criteria for [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy") are met when there are either two or more unprovoked seizures occurring more than 24 hours apart, or one unprovoked seizure with a recurrence risk of at least 60% over the next 10 years based on clinical and diagnostic findings.

Causes and contexts for unprovoked seizures include:

* Structural brain abnormalities, such as [brain tumors](/wiki/Brain_tumor "Brain tumor"), malformations of cortical development, and chronic lesions from prior brain trauma.[[41]](#cite_note-Chen2022-41)
* Genetic epilepsies: mutations affecting neuronal excitability or network function. Examples include [Dravet syndrome](/wiki/Dravet_syndrome "Dravet syndrome"), [Lennox–Gastaut syndrome](/wiki/Lennox%E2%80%93Gastaut_syndrome "Lennox–Gastaut syndrome"), and [juvenile myoclonic epilepsy](/wiki/Juvenile_myoclonic_epilepsy "Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy").[[15]](#cite_note-Berkowitz2022-15)
* Infectious etiologies: sequelae of central nervous system infections, such as [neurocysticercosis](/wiki/Neurocysticercosis "Neurocysticercosis") or [viral encephalitis](/wiki/Viral_encephalitis "Viral encephalitis").[[42]](#cite_note-Vezzani2015-42)
* Metabolic disorders: inborn errors of metabolism or [mitochondrial diseases](/wiki/Mitochondrial_diseases "Mitochondrial diseases") affecting neuronal function.
* Immune-mediated epilepsies: such as [autoimmune encephalitis](/wiki/Autoimmune_encephalitis "Autoimmune encephalitis").
* Neurodegenerative dementia: such as [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease").
* Unknown etiologies: in some cases, no clear cause is found despite thorough investigation (termed idiopathic seizures).

## Mechanism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Mechanism")]

Seizures are the result of neuronal activity in the brain that is abnormal, excessive, and synchronized (also referred to as hypersynchronous).[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11) At a cellular level, they reflect a disruption of the normal balance between excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmission. Under healthy conditions, excitation (mainly mediated by the neurotransmitter [glutamate](/wiki/Glutamate "Glutamate")) and inhibition (primarily via the neurotransmitter [GABA](/wiki/GABA "GABA")) maintain cortical stability. An excess of excitation or a failure of inhibition can tip this balance, promoting hypersynchronous neuronal firing characteristic of seizures.[[43]](#cite_note-Tatti2016-43)[[44]](#cite_note-Sarlo2021-44)[[45]](#cite_note-Treiman2001-45) The transition from an interictal state (between seizures) to an ictal state (seizure) is known as [ictogenesis](/w/index.php?title=Ictogenesis&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ictogenesis (page does not exist)"). This process involves a cascade of physiological and network-level changes that lead to the sudden onset of pathological activity.

In provoked seizures (e.g., due to trauma, metabolic insults, or infections), acute disturbances in ionic gradients, neurotransmitter release, and neuronal membrane stability may temporarily lower the threshold for seizure activity.

Brief seizures, such as absence seizures lasting 5–10 seconds, do not cause observable brain damage.[[46]](#cite_note-Dingledine2014-46) More prolonged seizures have a higher risk of neuronal death.[[46]](#cite_note-Dingledine2014-46) Prolonged and recurrent seizures, such as status epilepticus, typically cause brain damage.[[46]](#cite_note-Dingledine2014-46) Scarring of brain tissue ([gliosis](/wiki/Gliosis "Gliosis")), neuronal death, and shrinking of areas of the brain ([atrophy](/wiki/Atrophy "Atrophy")) are linked to recurrent seizures.[[46]](#cite_note-Dingledine2014-46)[[47]](#cite_note-Epi2008p483-47) These changes may lead to the development of epilepsy, in a process called [epileptogenesis](/wiki/Epileptogenesis "Epileptogenesis").[[47]](#cite_note-Epi2008p483-47)

## Clinical evaluation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Clinical evaluation")]

The clinical evaluation after a seizure event involves confirming if the episode was epileptic in nature, determining its type and cause, and distinguishing it from other conditions that can mimic seizures. A careful clinical history and targeted investigations are essential.[[15]](#cite_note-Berkowitz2022-15)[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3)
The events preceding seizure onset, clinical signs observed during the episode, and symptoms following the episode are critical for accurately classifying the seizure type. Individuals with focal preserved consciousness seizures may be able to recall detailed information about their seizures. However, many individuals cannot recall the details of their own seizures, so obtaining eyewitness accounts is often essential for an accurate diagnosis.[[15]](#cite_note-Berkowitz2022-15)[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3)[[48]](#cite_note-Rowland2022-48) When available, video recordings can provide valuable supplementary information, particularly in distinguishing epileptic seizures from mimics such as psychogenic nonepileptic seizures.[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3)

### Clinical history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Clinical history")]

A detailed seizure history should include information on pre-ictal, ictal, and post-ictal stages, as described below:

**Pre-ictal**

* Symptoms (auras), such as unusual sensations, déjà vu, or fear
* Triggers such as intense emotions, loud noise, flashing lights, exercise, menstruation, lack of sleep, fever, pregnancy, or stress

**Ictal**

* Semiology including motor activity, level of consciousness, automatisms, or autonomic signs
* Time of occurrence (during sleep or wakefulness)
* Length of episode (usually lasting minutes)
* Injuries sustained during seizures
* Witness accounts of the events

**Post-ictal**

* Postictal symptoms (e.g. confusion or drowsiness) that improve over time
* Focal neurologic deficits (Todd's paralysis)

Obtaining medical history is also important, including the following:

* Past history of neurological or systemic conditions
* Prior treatment history, including medications, responses, and side effects[[49]](#cite_note-49)
* History of head trauma
* Family history of seizures or epilepsy
* Developmental history in children
* History of febrile seizures
* Use of medications, alcohol, or illicit substances

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Bittentongue.JPG/250px-Bittentongue.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bittentongue.JPG)

An individual who has bitten the tip of their tongue while having a seizure

### Physical examination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Physical examination")]

A focused neurological examination can yield additional diagnostic clues, particularly soon after a seizure. Findings may include:[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3)

* Tongue or oral injuries, such as lateral tongue bites, which strongly suggest a generalized tonic–clonic seizure, though they occur in only about one-third of cases
* Postictal focal neurological signs, such as weakness or asymmetric reflexes
* Urinary or fecal incontinence, which, while not specific, can support the diagnosis of a generalized seizure

Between seizures, the neurological examination is often normal.[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3)

### Laboratory tests

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Laboratory tests")]

Laboratory testing is often performed in the evaluation of a new-onset seizure, particularly when a provoked cause is suspected.[[15]](#cite_note-Berkowitz2022-15) Common investigations include:

* Serum glucose: to rule out hypoglycemia
* Electrolytes (sodium, calcium, magnesium): to identify metabolic disturbances[[7]](#cite_note-Winkel2022-7)
* Renal and hepatic function panels: to assess for organ dysfunction
* Toxicology screening: to detect alcohol, illicit substances, or prescription drug toxicity
* Infection markers (e.g., complete blood count, inflammatory markers): when infection is suspected
* Lactate: elevation in lactate levels within the first two hours after seizure onset is associated with generalized seizure, though this is not a requirement for diagnosis[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Laboratory findings can help identify treatable causes of seizures and guide management decisions.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/EEG_Recording_Cap.jpg/250px-EEG_Recording_Cap.jpg)](/wiki/File:EEG_Recording_Cap.jpg)

An EEG can aid in locating the focus of the epileptic seizure.

### Electroencephalography (EEG)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Electroencephalography (EEG)")]

An **electroencephalogram (EEG)** records electrical activity in the brain and can help support a diagnosis of epilepsy. Interictal EEG (which can be observed between seizure episodes) may reveal epileptiform abnormalities, such as spikes, sharp waves, or spike-and-wave discharges. However, an unremarkable EEG does not exclude epilepsy.

In certain cases, prolonged video EEG monitoring is used to capture seizures in real time and clarify seizure type, localization, or the diagnosis when psychogenic nonepileptic seizures are suspected.

EEG monitoring is frequently performed in the inpatient setting within a hospital's epilepsy monitoring unit (EMU), but in some cases, portable EEGs may be used in the outpatient setting for seizure monitoring.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Neuroimaging

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Neuroimaging")]

Brain imaging is recommended in most cases of new-onset unprovoked seizures to identify structural abnormalities that may predispose to epilepsy. Imaging techniques include:[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3)

* [Magnetic resonance imaging](/wiki/Magnetic_resonance_imaging "Magnetic resonance imaging") (MRI): the preferred modality for detecting cortical dysplasia, tumors, mesial temporal sclerosis, and other lesions
* [Computed tomography](/wiki/CT_scan "CT scan") (CT): often used in emergency settings to exclude acute hemorrhage or trauma

Adult patients with first-time seizures who have a suspected intracranial lesion or red-flag symptoms (e.g. focal deficits, altered mental status, fever, severe headache) should immediately receive a noncontrast head CT.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

When CT findings are normal in patients with a first unprovoked seizure, a follow-up MRI is recommended because it may detect epileptogenic lesions not visible on CT.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Differential diagnosis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Differential diagnosis")]

Several conditions can mimic epileptic seizures and must be considered, including the following:

* [Syncope](/wiki/Syncope_(medicine) "Syncope (medicine)") (transient loss of consciousness due to cerebral hypoperfusion)
* [Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures](/wiki/Psychogenic_non-epileptic_seizure "Psychogenic non-epileptic seizure") (PNES)
* [Transient ischemic attacks](/wiki/Transient_ischemic_attacks "Transient ischemic attacks") (TIAs)
* [Paroxysmal movement disorders](/wiki/Paroxysmal_attack "Paroxysmal attack")
* [Migraine aura](/wiki/Migraine_aura "Migraine aura")
* [Panic attack](/wiki/Panic_attack "Panic attack")
* [Narcolepsy](/wiki/Narcolepsy "Narcolepsy") with [cataplexy](/wiki/Cataplexy "Cataplexy")

Differentiating these conditions from epileptic seizures relies on careful history-taking, examination, EEG findings, and, when necessary, additional cardiac, metabolic, or psychiatric evaluations.

### Cincinnati method

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Cincinnati method")]

The "Cincinnati method" involves asking the patient repeatedly, immediately after the seizure to read a single sentence shown on a card.[[54]](#cite_note-:13-54) It can be used to lateralize the seizure, and is extremely cost-effective to administer.[[54]](#cite_note-:13-54) The method was invented in 1988 at the [Cincinnati University Hospital](/w/index.php?title=Cincinnati_University_Hospital&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cincinnati University Hospital (page does not exist)") in [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati"), Ohio.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

## Management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Management")]

Management of seizures depends on the clinical context, including whether the seizure is isolated or part of an ongoing epileptic disorder, and whether it is provoked or unprovoked.

### First aid during a seizure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: First aid during a seizure")]

Basic first aid during a tonic-clonic seizure focuses on ensuring the person's safety and preventing injury:[[56]](#cite_note-Asadi-Pooya2022-56)

* Protect the person: Gently guide them to the ground if they are standing, and remove sharp or dangerous objects nearby.
* Do not restrain movements: Allow the seizure to occur without attempting to hold the person down.
* Do not place objects in the mouth: This can cause choking or injury (tongue swallowing during a seizure is a myth)[[57]](#cite_note-57)
* Turn onto the side: Once convulsions stop, or if vomiting occurs, gently roll the person onto their side into the recovery position to maintain an open airway and prevent aspiration.
* Stay calm and reassure: Stay with the person until they have regained full awareness.
* Time the seizure: If it lasts longer than 5 minutes, call emergency services.[[58]](#cite_note-:11-58)

Emergency services should also be called if a person has multiple consecutive seizures, has trouble breathing or awakening afterward, becomes injured, is submerged in water, has a first-time seizure, has diabetes, or is pregnant.[[58]](#cite_note-:11-58)

For nonconvulsive seizures (such as absence seizures or focal impaired consciousness seizures), active physical first aid is often unnecessary. In these cases, observers should ensure the person is safe from harm, gently guide them away from danger if needed, and offer support and reassurance as they regain awareness.

### Emergency medical treatment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Emergency medical treatment")]

If a convulsive seizure lasts longer than five minutes or if repeated seizures occur without full recovery between events, the situation is classified as status epilepticus, a medical emergency requiring rapid intervention.[[59]](#cite_note-59) In emergency care, the first-line therapy for status epilepticus is the administration of a [benzodiazepine](/wiki/Benzodiazepine "Benzodiazepine") to terminate the seizure, with most guidelines recommending [lorazepam](/wiki/Lorazepam "Lorazepam"), [midazolam](/wiki/Midazolam "Midazolam") or [diazepam](/wiki/Diazepam "Diazepam"). Early benzodiazepine treatment is associated with better seizure control and improved outcomes.[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3) Even in shorter seizures that do not require use of benzodiazepines, intravenous access is usually established in case of multiple seizure episodes or seizure prolongation.

If seizures persist despite benzodiazepine administration (second-line therapy), an intravenous antiseizure medication is given. Recommended options include [fosphenytoin](/wiki/Fosphenytoin "Fosphenytoin"), [valproate](/wiki/Valproate "Valproate"), or [levetiracetam](/wiki/Levetiracetam "Levetiracetam"), depending on patient-specific factors and institutional protocols.[[60]](#cite_note-DeWaele2013-60) In cases of refractory status epilepticus (seizures continuing despite first- and second-line treatments), patients typically require intensive care unit management. This involves continuous EEG monitoring and administration of anesthetic agents such as propofol or continuous infusion of midazolam.[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3)

Prompt recognition and treatment of status epilepticus are critical to prevent permanent neuronal injury, systemic complications, and death.

### Management of provoked seizures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Management of provoked seizures")]

If a seizure is provoked by an acute reversible cause, treatment focuses on addressing the underlying condition. Long-term antiseizure medications are typically not needed once the acute cause has been resolved, unless seizures recur.

### Management after unprovoked seizures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Management after unprovoked seizures")]

After a first unprovoked seizure, management depends on assessing the risk of recurrence. Antiseizure medication may be considered after a single event if risk factors for epilepsy are identified, such as epileptiform abnormalities on EEG or structural lesions on MRI. In other cases, careful observation may be appropriate.

### Long-term management of epilepsy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Long-term management of epilepsy")]

See also: [Epilepsy § Management](/wiki/Epilepsy#Management "Epilepsy")

Long-term management applies to individuals diagnosed with epilepsy. The goals are seizure control, minimizing adverse effects, and optimizing quality of life.**[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3)**

It is recommended to start with one anti-seizure medication.[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3)[[61]](#cite_note-Liu2017-61) Another may be added if one is not enough to control the seizure occurrence.[[61]](#cite_note-Liu2017-61) Approximately 70% of people can obtain full control with continuous use of medication.[[62]](#cite_note-WHO2012-62) The type of medication used is based on the type of seizure.[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3)[[61]](#cite_note-Liu2017-61)

Patients with acute repetitive seizures (also known as "seizure clusters") have recurrent seizures within 6-24 hours of the first episode and are at an increased risk of developing status epilepticus. They are often prescribed rescue benzodiazepines in the form of rectal gels or nasal sprays for at-home use during emergencies.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

Anti-seizure medications may be slowly stopped after some time if a person has just experienced one seizure and has not had any more.[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3) The decision to stop anti-seizure medications should be discussed between the doctor and patient, weighing the benefits and risks.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Epilepsy-surgery-in-progress.jpg/250px-Epilepsy-surgery-in-progress.jpg)](/wiki/File:Epilepsy-surgery-in-progress.jpg)

Doctor performing an epilepsy surgery at Jaslok Hospital, Mumbai.

In severe cases where seizures are uncontrolled by at least two anti-seizure medications, brain surgery can be a treatment option.[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3)[[61]](#cite_note-Liu2017-61) Epilepsy surgery is especially useful for those with focal seizures, where the seizures are coming from a specific part of the brain.[[61]](#cite_note-Liu2017-61) The amount of brain removed during the surgery depends on the extent of the brain involved in the seizures. It can range from just removing one lobe of the brain ([temporal lobectomy](/wiki/Anterior_temporal_lobectomy "Anterior temporal lobectomy")) to disconnecting an entire side of the brain ([hemispherectomy](/wiki/Hemispherectomy "Hemispherectomy")).[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3) The procedure can be curative, where seizures are eliminated.[[3]](#cite_note-Abou-Khalil2022-3) However, if it is not curative, it can be palliative, reducing the frequency of seizures but not eliminating them.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Another intervention for refractory epilepsy is [vagus nerve stimulation](/wiki/Vagus_nerve_stimulation "Vagus nerve stimulation") (VNS), which involves insertion of an electrical device that stimulates the left vagus nerve.[[65]](#cite_note-65) Its mechanism of action is not fully understood. VNS is contraindicated in patients with cardiac conduction problems, sleep apnea, and should be avoided in patients with programmable shunt valves.[[66]](#cite_note-66) The most common side effect associated with VNS is hoarseness, and side effects usually improve over time or with decreased levels of stimulation.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

### Diet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Diet")]

A [ketogenic diet](/wiki/Ketogenic_diet "Ketogenic diet") or [modified Atkins diet](/wiki/Modified_Atkins_diet "Modified Atkins diet") may help in those who have epilepsy who do not improve following typical treatments, with evidence for its effectiveness growing.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) The diet leads to an increased elevation of plasma [decanoic acid](/wiki/Capric_acid "Capric acid") and [ketones](/wiki/Ketone "Ketone"). However, some research is giving the indication that it is the decanoic acid that is anti-convulsant.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

#### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Other")]

Helmets may be used to protect the head during a seizure. Some claim that [seizure response dogs](/wiki/Seizure_response_dog "Seizure response dog"), a form of [service dog](/wiki/Service_dog "Service dog"), can predict seizures.[[71]](#cite_note-Doh2007-71) Evidence for this, however, is poor.[[71]](#cite_note-Doh2007-71) [Cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)") has also been used for the management of seizures that do not respond to anti-seizure medications. Research on its effectiveness is ongoing, but current research shows that it does reduce seizure frequency.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73)

## Prognosis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Prognosis")]

The prognosis after a first seizure depends on the underlying cause, seizure type, and patient-specific factors. In general, individuals who experience a single provoked seizure due to an acute and reversible cause (such as hypoglycemia or head trauma) have a low risk of recurrence once the underlying issue is treated. Following a first unprovoked seizure, the risk of more seizures in the next two years is around 40%.[[74]](#cite_note-:0-74) Starting anti-seizure medications reduces the recurrence of seizures by 35% within the first two years. The greatest predictors of more seizures are problems either on the EEG or on imaging of the brain. Those with normal EEG and normal physical exam following a first unprovoked seizure had less risk of recurrence in the next two years, with a risk of 25%. In adults, after 6 months of being seizure-free after a first seizure, the risk of a subsequent seizure in the next year is less than 20% regardless of treatment.[[75]](#cite_note-75) The prognosis of epilepsy depends on seizure type, cause, and treatment response. About two-thirds of patients achieve seizure control with medication, while surgery or [neuromodulation](/wiki/Neuromodulation "Neuromodulation") may help those with drug-resistant epilepsy.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

## Epidemiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Epidemiology")]

Seizures are relatively common neurological events, with an estimated lifetime risk of experiencing at least one seizure approaching 8-10% within the general population. However, not all seizures are indicative of epilepsy; numerous episodes are provoked by transient factors such as infections, metabolic abnormalities, or trauma. In adults, the risk of seizure recurrence within the five years following a new-onset seizure is 35%; the risk rises to 75% in persons who have had a second seizure.[[77]](#cite_note-Gavvala20162-77) In children, the risk of seizure recurrence within the five years following a single unprovoked seizure is about 50%; the risk rises to about 80% after two unprovoked seizures.[[78]](#cite_note-78) In the United States in 2011, seizures resulted in an estimated 1.6 million emergency department visits; approximately 400,000 of these visits were for new-onset seizures.[[77]](#cite_note-Gavvala20162-77)

Global variations exist, with higher rates of seizures and epilepsy reported in regions with elevated prevalence of risk factors such as central nervous system infections, traumatic brain injury, and limited access to perinatal care. Seizures contribute significantly to the global burden of neurological disease, affecting individuals' quality of life, social participation, and access to education and employment.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: History")]

Seizures have been recognized throughout recorded history. Early descriptions date back to ancient [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") around 2000 BCE, where seizures were often attributed to supernatural causes or demonic possession.[[79]](#cite_note-Ali2015-79)[[80]](#cite_note-Magiorkinis2009-80) Similar beliefs persisted across ancient cultures, including in Egypt, India, and Greece.[[79]](#cite_note-Ali2015-79) In the 5th century BCE, the physician [Hippocrates](/wiki/Hippocrates "Hippocrates") challenged supernatural explanations in his treatise *[On the Sacred Disease](/wiki/On_the_Sacred_Disease "On the Sacred Disease")*, proposing that epilepsy was a disorder of the brain.[[79]](#cite_note-Ali2015-79) However, stigma surrounding seizures remained widespread for centuries.

## Society and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Society and culture")]

### Economics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Economics")]

Seizures result in direct economic costs of about one billion dollars in the United States.[[6]](#cite_note-AFP2012-6) Epilepsy results in economic costs in Europe of around €15.5 billion in 2004.[[81]](#cite_note-NChp1-81) In India, epilepsy is estimated to result in costs of US$1.7 billion or 0.5% of the GDP.[[62]](#cite_note-WHO2012-62) They make up about 1% of emergency department visits (2% for emergency departments for children) in the United States.[[82]](#cite_note-emcna2011-82)

## Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seizure&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Research")]

Much of recent epilepsy research has been focused on identifying causes for seizures, predicting them, and preventing their onset.[[83]](#cite_note-:3-83)[[84]](#cite_note-:4-84)

Some types of seizures occur due to genetic mutations, such as those found in ion channels.[[85]](#cite_note-:12-85) Current genetic epilepsy research is aimed at identifying genetic risk factors, understanding mutations, and developing genetic therapies. The development of [next-generation sequencing](/wiki/Massive_parallel_sequencing "Massive parallel sequencing") has significantly advanced the understanding of gene-driven epilepsies.[[85]](#cite_note-:12-85) Presently, there are experiments using [stem cells](/wiki/Stem_cell "Stem cell") for the study and management of genetic epilepsy.

Seizure prediction is a special case of seizure detection in which the developed systems can issue a warning before the clinical onset of the epileptic seizure.[[86]](#cite_note-:2-86)[[84]](#cite_note-:4-84) Recently, [biomarkers](/wiki/Biomarker "Biomarker") have been used for seizure prediction and prevention.[[85]](#cite_note-:12-85) These are methods of measuring changes that occur in patients with seizures to differentiate them from people without seizures. One example of biomarkers is the use of microelectrodes, which can be used to monitor abnormal electrical brain activity. Another example is diffusion tensor imaging MRI, which can show differences in structural connectivity in individuals with epilepsy.[[85]](#cite_note-:12-85)[[87]](#cite_note-87)

There are ongoing investigations to identify mechanisms to treat seizures, with the most promising recent discoveries involving the [mTOR](/wiki/MTOR "MTOR") pathway and the [cytokine](/wiki/Cytokine "Cytokine") (small signaling protein) [IL-1ß](/wiki/Interleukin_1_beta "Interleukin 1 beta").[[85]](#cite_note-:12-85) Several other structures and cells are also the topic of study, including cellular membrane proteins, components of the blood-brain barrier, glial cells, and antibodies.[[85]](#cite_note-:12-85) For treatment of genetic disorders, a process called [transfection](/wiki/Transfection "Transfection") involves the use of vectors to deliver pieces of genetic material to areas of the brain involved in seizure onset.[[83]](#cite_note-:3-83)

[Computational neuroscience](/wiki/Computational_neuroscience "Computational neuroscience") has brought a new perspective on seizures by considering the dynamical aspects.[[88]](#cite_note-Depannemaecker_4–8-88)
